summary: I was born in Atlanta in 1902, just a few blocks from the Biltmore. Growing up in Atlanta was important to me, especially since my grandfather, Evan Howell, was the founder and publisher of the Atlanta newspaper. My early years in Atlanta shaped much of my perspective, even though I never went into the newspaper business like some of my family. Later, after my studies, I returned to Atlanta to work with the Commission on Interracial Cooperation.

I was born and raised in Georgia, specifically in Atlanta, and attended public schools there before going to the University of Georgia. My family had deep roots in the state, with my grandfather on my father's side being a farmer in Washington, Georgia. My experiences in Georgia, including witnessing racial violence near Athens, deeply influenced my views and later work in racial cooperation.

Clark Howell was my mother's brother, and I was named after him. He was the son of my grandfather Evan Howell, who was the founder and publisher of the Atlanta newspaper. Although Clark Howell was a significant figure in my family, I did not follow his path into the newspaper business.

Evan Howell was my grandfather on my mother's side and the founder and publisher of the Atlanta newspaper. His influence was part of my family heritage, though I personally did not pursue a career in journalism. His legacy was well-known in Atlanta and shaped the environment I grew up in.

I attended the University of Georgia starting around 1916 or 1917 and graduated in 1921 at the age of 19. My experience there was fairly ordinary academically, focusing on liberal arts like Latin and Greek. However, a pivotal moment was living at the Chi Phi fraternity house in Athens during a lynching incident, which profoundly affected me and my views on race and justice.

During my time at the University of Georgia, I lived in the Chi Phi fraternity house. It was there that I first heard about a horrific lynching near Athens, which deeply disturbed me. The fraternity house was a setting for many discussions and reflections on the racial tensions of the time.

Athens, Georgia, was the site of a brutal lynching that I witnessed indirectly while at the University of Georgia. The town was proud of its 'mob-proof' courthouse, yet a mob still gathered and burned a man to death outside the city. This event left a lasting impression on me and influenced my later commitment to racial justice.

While at Harvard in 1921-22, I was invited by Donald Culross Peattie to hear W. E. B. Du Bois speak at the Liberal Club. Du Bois made a great impression on me with his speech, marking my first significant break from the southern racial traditions I had grown up with. This experience was an important step in my evolving views on race and equality.

After graduating from the University of Georgia in 1921, I spent a year at Harvard University. It was there that I met people like Donald Culross Peattie and Corliss Lamont, and attended events such as W. E. B. Du Bois's speech. Harvard was a place where I began to question southern traditions and expand my worldview before traveling to Europe.

At Harvard, I lived across from Donald Culross Peattie, who later became a noted botanical writer. He invited me to hear W. E. B. Du Bois speak, which was a pivotal moment for me. Peattie was part of the intellectual circle that helped me break away from some of my southern prejudices.

I met Corliss Lamont during my year at Harvard. He was part of the progressive circle I encountered there, which challenged my southern upbringing. Lamont's company and ideas contributed to my growing awareness of social and racial issues.

Before traveling to Europe, my mother gave me a book by H. G. Wells, which I read on the boat to London. I was greatly impressed by his vision of a better society, especially the last chapter. I even managed to meet him in London, where he advised me on learning languages and studying at the London School of Economics, shaping my educational path.

After Harvard, I traveled to London in 1922 with money from my family to explore Europe on my own terms. In London, I met H. G. Wells and later attended the London School of Economics. The city was a place of intellectual growth and exposure to diverse ideas and people, including many students from around the world.

Following H. G. Wells's advice, I enrolled at the London School of Economics after spending some time in Germany and returning home due to my mother's illness. The school exposed me to a wide range of international students and ideas, including many African American students. It was there I read J. H. Oldham's book about the Commission on Interracial Cooperation, which inspired me to return to Atlanta and work for racial justice.

The First World War affected my education, particularly in language studies, as German was not taught during that time. This influenced my language skills and later decisions about where to study in Europe. The war's aftermath also shaped the intellectual climate I encountered in London and beyond.

During my meeting with H. G. Wells, he mentioned Thomas W. Lamont as an example of a banker who had done a lot of good. Although my family had hoped I might enter banking, I was not interested in following Lamont's path. This conversation helped clarify my own ambitions and values.

My father was the state director of the Mutual Benefit Life Insurance Company in Georgia. His position influenced our family's financial situation and decisions, including his initial reluctance but eventual support for my studies abroad. His role reflected the kind of respectable bourgeois background I came from.

While at the London School of Economics, I was assigned to review a book by J. H. Oldham. His book detailed the founding and work of the Commission on Interracial Cooperation in Atlanta, which I had never heard of before. This reading motivated me to return home and work for the Commission, marking a turning point in my career.

After reading about the Commission on Interracial Cooperation in J. H. Oldham's book, I decided to return to Atlanta to work for it. My father arranged for me to meet Dr. Ashby Jones, the head, and then Will Alexander, the director. Although initially unpaid, I soon received a salary and was proud to contribute to racial cooperation efforts in my hometown.

Dr. Ashby Jones was the head of the Commission on Interracial Cooperation in Atlanta when I returned to work there. My father knew him well and arranged for me to meet him. Dr. Jones was kind but directed me to see Will Alexander, the director, to discuss employment.

Will Alexander was the director of the Commission on Interracial Cooperation when I sought to work there. Although the Commission initially had no budget to pay me, I offered to work without salary until January. To my surprise, they found funds and I began earning a good salary for the time, which was my first income.

Augusta, Georgia, was not directly mentioned in my story, but as a city in Georgia, it was part of the broader context of the state I grew up in. My experiences and work in racial cooperation were primarily centered in Atlanta and Athens, but the issues I confronted were relevant throughout Georgia, including places like Augusta.
